title: Setting Up Your Dev Environment
intro: A good dev environment saves you hours of frustration. Swipe right on solid setup practices, left on habits that will slow you down.

teach.title: Your Workspace Matters
teach.body: Before you write a single line of code, your dev environment needs to be ready. This means having Node.js installed, a good code editor (VS Code is the go-to), and a terminal you're comfortable with. Skipping this step is like trying to cook in a kitchen with no knives.

teach.title: A Chef's Mise en Place
teach.body: In professional kitchens, chefs practice 'mise en place' — everything in its place before you cook. Ingredients are prepped, tools are laid out, stations are clean. Setting up your dev environment is your mise en place. You chop the onions (install Node), sharpen the knives (configure VS Code), and preheat the oven (set up Git) BEFORE you start cooking code.

teach.title: The Orchestra's Sheet Music
teach.body: Imagine an orchestra where every musician plays from a different version of the sheet music. The violins are in one key, the cellos in another, and the drummer is playing a completely different song. Config files like `.nvmrc` and `.editorconfig` are your team's shared sheet music — they make sure everyone is playing the same tune, in the same key, at the same tempo.

teach.title: Consistency Is Key
teach.body: Professional teams use tools like `.nvmrc` files to lock the Node.js version, `.editorconfig` for consistent formatting, and `package.json` scripts so every developer runs the same commands. These small files prevent the classic 'works on my machine' problem.

A clean dev environment means you can go from idea to working prototype in hours, not days. When you spot an opportunity to build something people want, speed is everything — and your setup is the launchpad.

Invest 30 minutes setting up your environment properly and you'll save hours debugging weird issues later. Use a version manager for Node.js, pick one editor and learn it well, and always check that your tools are up to date.

cards.content: Use nvm or fnm to manage your Node.js version so you can switch between projects easily
cards.explanation: Version managers let you pin the exact Node.js version per project. Different projects may need different versions, and a version manager makes switching painless.

cards.content: Install all your project dependencies globally with npm install -g so they're available everywhere
cards.explanation: Global installs cause version conflicts between projects. Always install project dependencies locally with npm install so each project gets the versions it needs.

cards.content: Install ESLint and Prettier extensions in VS Code for automatic code formatting and error catching
cards.explanation: These extensions catch errors as you type and keep your code formatted consistently. They pay for themselves within minutes.

cards.content: Skip configuring your git username and email because you can always do it later
cards.explanation: Git won't let you commit without a name and email. Setting this up first avoids a confusing error message the moment you try to make your first commit.

cards.content: Run 'node --version' and 'npm --version' to verify everything is installed correctly before starting a project
cards.explanation: A quick version check takes 5 seconds and can save you from mysterious build errors caused by outdated or missing tools.

cards.content: Download whatever Node.js version pops up first on Google and install it without checking compatibility
cards.explanation: Next.js requires specific minimum Node.js versions. Grabbing a random version can lead to cryptic errors. Always check the docs for the recommended version.

cards.content: Add an .nvmrc file to your project so teammates automatically use the correct Node.js version
cards.explanation: An .nvmrc file documents the exact Node.js version your project needs. When a teammate runs 'nvm use', it picks up the right version automatically.
